Hearing of the wonderful things DR. SHEA was doing, I resolved to consult him. He told me my husband was alive; told me he would come home and when. My joy at all of it came true. He is home now, came back like he said he would. But this month I lost the sum of $250. I am a poor woman and I was almost insane. I went to DR. SHEA and he told me what to do. I did it as he told me. I thank God there is a man so gifted in our midst that can help people and tell them that to do. Signed, MARY J. H. TWELVE, 122 Third street, South Brooklyn.”

target: How Grasshoppers Propagate. A Truckee Meadows ranchman, who has been studying the ways of the grasshopper for the past two years, says they have a touch of intelligence in them that must come from the devil. Among many things he tells about the hoppers in proof of their being endowed with fiendish smartness, his description of the manner in which the eggs of the female are planted in the ground is not the least curious. He says when the time for depositing the eggs arrives the hoppers select a patch of ground that is somewhat soft, yet of firm texture. Here collect swarms of both males and females. The males set to work and bore a hole in the ground to the depth of about an inch, carefully smoothing the sides and rounding it. Five or six of them then seize upon a female and stand her on end in this hole, waist deep. They then carefully tamp in fine dirt all about the lower part of her body, and thus securely fasten her in the ground from the waist up. When

A Crime of the Age
Scared by a rumor, Jay Johnson C. Whittaker filled a place which for a man in his teens is justly regarded as one of the most enviable in the republic, a Cadet at West Point. Looked upon as the sole representative of six millions of the most loyal people, and personally popular with all who see manhood under a tawny skin, he received their considerate regard and best wishes. Prepossessing in appearance, scholarly, and refined, he moved in a social atmosphere that kindled the liveliest hopes for a glorious future.

target: DRINKS FOR FEVER PATIENTS.—Drinks made from fresh or preserved fruits are sometimes useful in fevers. Rhubarb tea is a very refreshing spring beverage; Slice about two pounds of rhubarb and boil for a quarter of an hour in a quart of water; strain the liquor in a jug, adding a small quantity of lemon peel and some sugar to taste; when cold it is fit for use. Apple water may be made in the same manner. The apples should be peeled and cored. Sugar should not be added to either of the above until after the liquor is removed from the fire. In the absence of fresh fruit, a pleasant beverage may be prepared by stirring a sufficient raspberry jam or currant jelly into the required quantity of water, straining the liquor before giving it to the patient.

target: We regard the Independent movements in Georgia, Tennessee, North Carolina and Virginia, only as a means to and end. We no longer love party organizations and partisans for themselves alone, but for the good they can do the race. We care no more for Senator Mahone than we care for any other bold and not over-scrupulous man who should vault himself into power by means of our suffrage. Hero-worship is a thing out of the question. All we ask is a just return for the support we give, and we do not want any more sentimental gush over the transaction than we would expect from a man who sold us a felt hat or a railroad ticket.

target: Beds.—Our beds are our night-clothes and ought to be kept as clean as our shirts and coats. Woolen blankets are healthier than quilts; put a heavy United States army blanket over a basin full of hot water and see how fast the heat makes its way through the wet; a quilt would stop it like an iron lid, and thus tend to check the exhalation of the human body. In order to clean a quilt you have first to loosen the pressed cotton; a woolen blanket can be streamed and dried in a couple of hours. For similar reasons a straw tick is better than a horsehair mattress, though a woven-wire mattress is perhaps preferable to both. Feather beds are a recognized nuisance. Children over 10 years should sleep alone, or at least under separate blankets, if the bedsteads do not reach ground.

For the first half of the week, in addition to the regular performance, an olio of old-time minstrel will be presented, not in connection with the performances, but as a special feature, introducing Anna Laughlin, Al Lamar and members of the company in up-to-date specialties.

target: Resolved, that we, the Afro-Americans of St. Paul, express our heartfelt thanks to the officers and members of the Fourteenth Minnesota volunteers for their humane assistance to the brave Afro-American volunteers when they were so brutally attacked by the First Georgia Volunteers at Knoxville, Tenn. Resolved, further, that a copy of these resolutions be sent to the officer in command of the Fourteenth Minnesota, and that they be published in each of the daily papers. A committee, consisting of J. H. Dillingham, W. T. Francis, George B. Lowe, Mrs. T. H. Lyle, and Miss Heckman was appointed to call on the commanding officer at Camp Van Duzer to present to him a copy of the resolutions.

target: Among the notable characters of the city of St. Paul few fill so unique a position as Sir Knight Andrew Jackson. He spent 30 of the 62 years of his life in slavery, and in 1862 landed in St. Paul. He was one of the seven who formed Pioneer Lodge No. 1, in 1866 and has been prominent in Masonic circles and a highly respected citizen ever since. Wednesday evening he invited a number of the good citizens of the city and fellow crafts men to a banquet at Masonic hall to celebrate the 36th anniversary of his arrival in the city and 32nd of the establishment of Masonry in Minnesota. When the invited guests arrived it was found that there were just 36, one for each year the host had lived among us. They found a table in the form of a Roman cross handsomely decorated with elegant ware and cut flowers and jaden with toothsome viands fit to tempt the palates of the veriest epicure. To-master T. H. Lyles sat at the head of the table with the honored host on the

input:
correct: CorFEYVILLE, Kan., Dec. 18.—Lizzie
Williams, an eighteen-year-old negro
girl whose home is at Kansas City, shot
and killed Ed Parks, a negro lad
about the same age, at noon Parks
was her sweetheart and was visiting
her at her boarding place here when
the fatal shot was fired. They had
been playing with an empty revolver
and she put two cartridges in it and
pointing it at him laughingly remaried
that she was going to shoot him. She
pulled the trigger and the ball entered
Parks' left breast, killing him instant-
ly. She claims that it was accidental,
as she didn't know the revolver was a
double-action one, and this is the gen-
eral opinion, as there was no trouble
between them.
target: COFFEYVILLE, Kan., Dec. 13.—Lizzie Williams, an eighteen-year-old negro girl whose home is at Kansas City, shot and killed Ed Parks, a negro lad about the same age, at noon. Parks was her sweetheart and was visiting her at her boarding place here when the fatal shot was fired. They had been playing with an empty revolver and she put two cartridges in it and pointing it at him laughingly remarked that she was going to shoot him. She pulled the trigger and the ball entered Parks' left breast, killing him instantly. She claims that it was accidental, as she didn't know the revolver was a double-action one, and this is the general opinion, as there was no trouble between them.
